# Questions in Livestream 212

Live Stream #212: One Degree of Separation

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:01</a> | Which version of Reliquary of the Dead is best to get in order to support you? | He says the softcover supports him best financially, while the hardcover has extra artwork. | He explains that the editions differ materially: the hardcover includes additional artwork and a more expensive presentation, but the softcover gives him the better direct return. He frames the choice as a tradeoff between collector value and financial support, not as a content difference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=1387" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:07</a> | Is there any record of Yahweh before the Pentateuch outside the Bible, perhaps under another name? | He says no secure pre-Pentateuch attestation is known; proposed earlier references are too ambiguous to be persuasive. | He distinguishes possible guesses from firm evidence. He says the earliest secure extrabiblical reference is still the Moabite Stone, while proposed earlier Egyptian or other references are too ambiguous or indirect to establish Yahweh with certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=1695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:15</a> | What is a biblical response to overrealized demonology? | He argues believers can be influenced but not possessed, and he rejects blaming ordinary sin or conflict on demons. | He describes overrealized demonology as a habit of treating demons as the explanation for nearly everything. His response is to take spiritual realities seriously without erasing human responsibility, ordinary temptation, bad choices, mental strain, or interpersonal conflict. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=2185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:25</a> | Does God condemn any specific economic system? | He says Scripture gives moral principles rather than a full system, but some systems fit those principles far worse than others. | He says Scripture sets principles such as justice, property rights, care for the poor, and limits on exploitation rather than prescribing a modern economic model. He then argues that systems which destroy those principles, especially totalizing communist models, can be judged by their results and assumptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=2713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:13</a> | How would you respond to the claim that Scandinavian welfare states are a successful rebuttal to criticism of communism? | He says Scandinavian countries are capitalist welfare states, not communist or socialist examples. | He argues that the Scandinavian examples still depend on capitalism, markets, private ownership, and a limited population willing to pay high taxes for social benefits. He treats them as welfare-state capitalism, not as evidence that communism works. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=2870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:50</a> | Why do you think Nimrod is an amalgamation, and of whom? | He treats Nimrod as a composite memory of Mesopotamian kings, but says the exact figures behind it remain uncertain. | He points to Nimrod's association with founding or ruling multiple Mesopotamian centers as a clue that the biblical figure may compress more than one royal memory. He does not identify a single certain source figure, but treats the tradition as a composite of famous ancient Near Eastern rulers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=2978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:38</a> | Do you agree with the reading of Proverbs 22:6 as letting a child have his own way? | No. He argues that reading does not fit the Hebrew and keeps the sense closer to training or instruction. | He spends time on the Hebrew wording and says the verb is still an instruction to train or dedicate the child. He rejects turning the verse into advice to let a child follow his own autonomous path, while noting that the exact phrasing is debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=3445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:25</a> | Did the ancient Egyptians ever need to go to war with ancient Sudan over stopping the Nile? | He says no; ancient states did not have the engineering capacity to shut down the Nile that way. | He contrasts modern dam disputes with ancient capabilities. In his view, ancient Nubian or Sudanese polities could not build the kind of infrastructure that would stop the Nile's flow, so Egypt would not have faced that specific strategic threat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=3606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:06</a> | Have Moabite palaces and fortresses been excavated? | Yes. He says major Moabite sites have been excavated, even if the full corpus is not easy to summarize quickly. | He says there is Moabite archaeology, including fortified and elite contexts, but he does not try to produce a complete site catalogue live. His answer is that the material exists, though it is not as simple as naming one definitive palace for the whole question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=3743" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:23</a> | Wouldn't a dam only delay the flow for a while? | He says a dam regulates release through stored water and floodgates, so it can do more than create a momentary delay. | He explains that a dam is not just a temporary obstruction: it creates storage capacity and allows controlled release. Because a reservoir can hold and meter water, it can affect downstream flow for longer than a simple blockage would. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=4000" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:40</a> | Is Judges 1:27 a problem for a 19th-dynasty conquest model? | He says no; leftover garrison centers could persist after a broader conquest and then decline later. | He argues that a conquest model does not require every Egyptian-linked center to disappear immediately. Garrison towns or administrative pockets could remain while the larger political situation changed, so Judges 1:27 is not automatically fatal to the model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=4491" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:51</a> | Could the Egyptian material culture in question just be elite emulation? | He says elite emulation alone does not explain the broader pattern of ordinary material culture and administration. | He allows that elite emulation might explain some prestigious objects, but says the evidence under discussion is broader than imitation by local elites. For him, administrative features and common material patterns point to actual Egyptian presence or influence, not only fashion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=4571" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:11</a> | Is Dallas Theological Seminary a good school? | He gives a mixed answer: strong in languages and some cognate work, weaker in other areas. | He says Dallas can be very strong in elite language training and related biblical studies areas, but he distinguishes that from the whole institution or every program. His recommendation depends on what side of the school and what academic goals the student has. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=4713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:33</a> | What inspired you to pursue an MD? | He says pastoral interests and a shift away from Unix and systems work pushed him toward ministry training. | He explains that he had reached a point where continuing in Unix and systems work was not appealing, while ministry and pastoral questions had become more important to him. The MD path fit that vocational turn, even though he also describes the training as demanding. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=5032" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:52</a> | Don't all pastors need a degree in used car sales nowadays? | He says the joke is uncomfortably close to his experience reviewing pastoral resumes, including many with weak or false credentials. | He riffs on the joke by saying he has seen many applicants move from sales or unrelated work into pastoral roles with thin theological preparation. His concern is not the prior job itself, but weak training, inflated resumes, and churches treating salesmanship as ministry qualification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=5449" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:49</a> | Should pastors use AI to write their sermons? | He rejects that strongly and treats AI sermon-writing as a bad substitute for real study and exegesis. | He says sermon preparation requires the pastor to read, interpret, and apply the text rather than outsource that work. He is not opposed to AI as a tool in every context, but rejects using it to generate sermons because it bypasses the pastoral and exegetical labor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=6023" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:23</a> | Do you have a favorite novel? | Yes. He names Dune as his favorite novel. | He answers directly that Dune is his favorite novel and does not build a long list of alternatives. The transcript supports only the direct preference, with brief affirmation rather than extended literary analysis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=6048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:48</a> | What books do you recommend on the spirit world? | He points viewers to the bibliography he already posted on his community page rather than rebuilding the list live. | He says he has already posted bibliographies and lecture-related resources on the spirit world, so the practical recommendation is to consult those existing lists. He does not recreate all titles in the livestream answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=6264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:24</a> | What does Selah mean in Psalms? | He says the exact sense is uncertain and common suggestions remain educated guesses. | He treats Selah as an uncertain musical or liturgical marker rather than a word with a settled translation. The answer emphasizes caution: scholars can offer proposals, but the exact meaning is not firmly recoverable from the evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=6298" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:58</a> | What time of day did people typically bathe in the ancient Near East? | He says ritual purpose mattered more than clock time, so practice varied with context. | He says ordinary and ritual bathing depended on circumstance rather than a universal daily schedule. He mentions that evening or sunset could make practical sense in some settings, but the main point is contextual variation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=6469" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:49</a> | Did they practice pouring rather than full immersion? | Generally yes in the bathing context under discussion; he describes pouring as the normal pattern. | He says the relevant ancient bathing practice was usually pouring water over the body, not full immersion. He connects that to water jars and ritual washing imagery, where people draw or pour water rather than submerge themselves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=6564" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:24</a> | Did Christians copy Egyptian practices? | He says ritual practices shared a common ancient Near Eastern language, so similarities are not simple plagiarism. | He argues that similar ritual forms can arise from a shared cultural environment rather than direct copying. His answer pushes against simplistic dependence claims by describing a wider pool of ancient Near Eastern practices and symbols. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=6988" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:28</a> | What do you think of Kagemusha? | He says it is good, though not among his very top films. | He places Kagemusha positively within Kurosawa's work, but says he prefers films such as Rashomon, Seven Samurai, and Yojimbo. The answer is appreciative but ranks it below his favorites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=7125" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:45</a> | What do you think of the recent Myth Vision podcast videos? | He is cautious and says he is still judging the situation rather than giving a firm verdict. | He says he is still watching how the situation develops, especially whether apologies and changes are substantive. He criticizes prior slanderous behavior but avoids declaring a final judgment in the moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=7187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:47</a> | Why wouldn't I fit into Moody? | He jokes that Moody's legalistic culture would be restrictive and says even appearance standards would be an issue. | He answers humorously that Moody would object to visible markers such as facial hair and would feel restrictive. The broader point is that he associates the institution with a highly controlled, legalistic culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=7461" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:21</a> | Was he part of the crowd that got your library privileges revoked? | He says yes, and says the consequences went beyond library privileges to lost academic and publication opportunities. | He says the episode was not merely about access to a library. In his account, the accusations affected his ability to work academically, publish, and maintain professional relationships, so the damage was broader than a single institutional privilege. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=7621" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:01</a> | Does that sound like lawsuit-level stuff? | He says they considered it, but jurisdiction and cost made a lawsuit impractical. | He says the issue looked serious enough that legal action was considered, but pursuing a case would have required money and jurisdictional footing they did not have. The answer stresses practical barriers rather than saying the conduct was harmless. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=7800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:00</a> | Who proved it was slander? | He credits Pine Creek with checking the full context and challenging the accusation. | He says Pine Creek listened to the relevant context and publicly challenged the accusation, forcing attention back onto what was actually said. He treats that as the key public vindication, while also noting others were involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=7916" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:56</a> | Didn't your pastor also do a video? | He says yes, but credits Pine Creek with spearheading the public vindication. | He acknowledges that his pastor also made a video, but says Pine Creek was the one who drove the main public response. The distinction is between supportive participation and the person he sees as leading the challenge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=8151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:51</a> | How has it been finding academic work in the two years since you were both fired? | He says it was effectively impossible at first, though conditions have improved somewhat more recently. | He says the immediate aftermath made academic work essentially closed off, especially because of reputational damage and institutional risk. More recently he has seen some improvement, but he still presents the situation as deeply difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=8321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:41</a> | Since going full-time for a while now, have things been stable for you all? | He says it has been up and down financially, though they are continuing the channel's work and seeing some hopeful changes. | He describes full-time channel work as financially uneven rather than fully stable. The transcript supports a mixed answer: they have had hard stretches, but they are continuing, adapting, and seeing signs that things may improve. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=8451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:51</a> | Do you have any academic opportunities anymore? | He says no formal opportunities remain, even though the books still circulate in academic settings. | He says he does not have formal academic opportunities at this point, but his published work is still being used or discussed in academic contexts. That creates a distinction between institutional position and continuing scholarly influence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=8909" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:29</a> | Wasn't Reuben disqualified as firstborn? | He says Reuben was effectively displaced rather than formally stripped in a simple legal sense. | He explains that Reuben loses the practical privileges associated with firstborn status, especially as leadership and blessing move elsewhere. He avoids reducing the matter to a neat formal legal category and instead describes a functional displacement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=8972" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:32</a> | What did Ruth do to Boaz in Ruth 3? | He treats it as an intimate but honorable marriage overture, not as a crude or pornographic scene. | He walks through Ruth approaching Boaz at the threshing floor, uncovering his feet, and asking him to spread his covering over her. He argues the scene is a bold marriage proposal and covenantal request, while rejecting both naive sanitizing and crude sexualized readings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=9588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:48</a> | Could a good drama film be made from Ruth? | Possibly yes, though he doubts it would become a mass-market hit. | He thinks Ruth could work as a serious drama because the story has tension, character, and relational stakes. His hesitation is commercial: he doubts a faithful drama would have broad blockbuster appeal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=9763" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:43</a> | Why did the Semites live in the temple district at Avaris? | He says the district was a religious center with Semitic temples, so it made sense for them to cluster there. | He connects the settlement pattern to religious and communal identity. If the district contained Semitic temples and cultic institutions, then living nearby would make practical sense for Semitic groups in Avaris. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=9812" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:32</a> | Can you elaborate on law versus order in the ancient Near East? | He contrasts Mesopotamian law-heavy framing with Egypt's stronger emphasis on social order and tradition. | He says Mesopotamian societies present themselves more through legal codes and formal law collections, while Egypt emphasizes ma'at, social order, and established tradition. The distinction is not that Egypt had no rules, but that its ideological framing was different. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=10046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:26</a> | If Noah and his wife had adult time, how would Noah know the father of Canaan? | He answers in straightforward biological terms: pregnancy would eventually reveal paternity within the story world. | He treats the question as a practical narrative issue rather than a theological puzzle. If the relevant act produced a pregnancy, the timing of conception and birth would eventually make the father of Canaan knowable in the story world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=10154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:14</a> | What is your opinion of constructed languages such as Esperanto? | He says they can be fun and useful, but natural languages will always dominate actual long-term use. | He says constructed languages can be interesting projects and may have limited usefulness, but they face the problem that real language communities form around history, culture, and daily use. For that reason, natural languages tend to outlast artificial universal-language efforts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=10657" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:37</a> | Why did the devil create calculus? | He jokes that calculus is evil, then says the heavy memorization of formulas was what made it difficult for him. | He answers in a joking register but explains the real frustration: he could grasp some concepts, yet the memorization load and formulas made calculus painful. The transcript supports humor plus personal academic struggle, not a serious theological claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=10985" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:05</a> | Is the KTU 1 phrase claimed as "son of El/Yahweh" valid, and does Ugaritic ignore silent he letters? | He is not convinced; he says Ugaritic does not ignore silent he letters and the proposed reading sounds stretched. | He says the proposed Ugaritic reading does not persuade him and that treating silent he letters as ignorable is not how the language works. His answer is cautious because he is responding live, but the basic judgment is that the claim looks forced. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=11204" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:44</a> | What are good topics of discussion for a young Christian adults group? | He recommends taking them deep into the Bible and helping them own the faith for themselves. | He says the goal should not be shallow youth-group-style topics, but serious biblical engagement that helps young adults make the faith their own. He stresses depth, honesty about difficult questions, and forming convictions rather than inherited slogans. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=11744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:44</a> | Is there evidence of YouTube shenanigans behind the channel's slower growth? | Yes. He thinks algorithmic distribution effects are real, though also fairly normal for the platform. | He says there is evidence that YouTube distribution affects the channel, but he frames it as the kind of platform behavior many channels face. The answer is yes, with the caveat that it is not necessarily a unique conspiracy against them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=11864" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:44</a> | Have you gotten help from Inspiring Philosophy or other larger Christian channels? | He says he has a friendly relationship with Inspiring Philosophy, but not broad backing from larger channels. | He says Inspiring Philosophy has been friendly and that there is some relationship there, but he does not describe major institutional support from large Christian channels. He also suggests some larger channels are cautious about associating publicly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=12141" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:21</a> | What is your best advice for someone entering grad school and aiming for academia? | He advises realism about institutional politics and says survival often depends on keeping your head down. | He gives a sober warning that graduate school and academia involve politics, incentives, and pressure to conform. His practical advice is to understand the system, avoid unnecessary conflict, and recognize that intellectual ability alone does not guarantee survival. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=12594" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:54</a> | How did Kenneth Kitchen get through academic politics like that? | He says Kitchen largely bulldozed through it by being indifferent to collegial games and incentives. | He portrays Kitchen as unusually resilient because he did not play the normal prestige and approval games. Kitchen's productivity, independence, and willingness to absorb criticism let him keep working despite opposition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=12716" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:56</a> | What about the treaty parallels Kitchen argued for? | He says Kitchen took criticism there too because those arguments cut against dominant consensus positions. | He says Kitchen's treaty-parallel arguments drew pushback because they challenged prevailing assumptions about biblical chronology and ancient Near Eastern parallels. The answer presents the criticism as part of the same broader academic resistance Kitchen faced. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=12980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:20</a> | Don't controversial books sell well? | He says controversy alone does not sell books; sales depend more on channels, publicity, writing, and presentation. | He argues that controversy can attract attention but does not automatically create sales. Distribution, marketing, author platform, writing quality, and packaging matter more than simply being provocative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=13195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:55</a> | What is your favorite dinosaur, and why? | He names a small feathered favorite first, then lands more broadly on spinosaur-type dinosaurs as especially appealing. | He first mentions a smaller feathered dinosaur, then broadens to spinosaur-type dinosaurs as especially interesting. The answer is casual and preference-based rather than an argument from paleontology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=13267" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:07</a> | What is the difference between a pastor and a priest, and did pastor come from the Reformation? | He says pastor means shepherding role, priest implies a different sacramental function, and pastor is not merely a Reformation invention. | He explains that pastor refers to shepherding and oversight, while priest language usually carries sacramental or mediating functions in traditions that use it. He also says the term pastor is older than the Reformation and should not be treated as a Reformation invention. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=13602" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:42</a> | Is the two-powers-in-heaven idea a valid Old Testament view? | He says it has some validity as an interpretive pattern, though he treats it carefully rather than absolutely. | He says the two-powers idea can be a valid way to describe some Old Testament patterns, especially where divine figures are distinguished yet associated. He also cautions against flattening the evidence or treating the later category as if it mechanically solves every passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=14189" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:29</a> | Did the ancient Egyptians think Osiris was a real figure in the shape of an animal? | He says Osiris was normally human-formed, with animal associations mainly through composite or incarnated forms. | He says Osiris is normally represented as a human-form deity. Animal associations appear in connected or incarnated forms, such as Osiris-Apis, but that is different from saying Egyptians primarily imagined Osiris himself as an animal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=14235" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:15</a> | Was reusing Avaris as a graveyard a sign of contempt for the city? | He says no; it is better read as practical reuse of abandoned space than symbolic hatred. | He argues that abandoned urban areas naturally get reused, including as burial space, when buildings fall down and the site is no longer functioning as a city. He does not see the graveyard reuse as evidence of contempt by itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4H4OC1XPI-U?t=14918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:38</a> | Is tracing the origins of Yahweh worship a waste of time? | He says it can be worthwhile, but present evidence sets hard limits on how far the inquiry can go. | He says the question is legitimate and worth asking, but the evidence runs out quickly. Without new inscriptions or clearer early sources, researchers can explore possibilities but should be honest about the limits of reconstruction. |
